failure_surface: Output cells contain datetime.datetime objects with incorrect number format (mm-dd-yy) instead of m/dd/yyyy format strings as specified in the task requirements.
root_cause: The agent assigned string values from strftime() to cells but openpyxl still displayed them as datetime objects because the underlying cell data remained as datetime type. The number_format setting alone cannot convert datetime objects to display as formatted date strings without changing the cell value type or ensuring proper Excel date storage.
minimal_fix_hypothesis: When processing date cells, verify whether the source data is already a datetime object vs. an ISO-formatted string. If it's a datetime object, extract the date component directly without reformatting via strftime(), then apply the correct Excel number_format for display. Do not assume all date inputs are strings.
evidence: Verification output shows: Row 2: datetime.datetime(2021, 4, 21, 0, 0) | Format: mm-dd-yy - demonstrating the cells store datetime objects not formatted strings
The expected output format was m/dd/yyyy per instruction (see existing A2 format), but actual format shown was mm-dd-yy
Code assigns formatted_date = dt.strftime('%m/%d/%Y') but cell.value still shows datetime.datetime object in verification

memory_items.title: Validate Cell Data Types Before Processing
memory_items.lesson: Always check whether cells contain datetime objects, strings, or numbers before applying transformation logic.
memory_items.generalization: Use isinstance() checks on cell.value to determine actual data types (datetime, str, int) rather than assuming one type based on task description.

memory_items.title: Excel Number Format Does Not Change Value Type
memory_items.lesson: Setting number_format on an openpyxl cell controls display only, not the underlying stored value type.
memory_items.generalization: To achieve consistent date display, ensure the cell.value matches the intended type (date-only datetime, or string) AND apply matching number_format.

memory_items.title: Avoid Unnecessary Type Conversions on Date Objects
memory_items.lesson: Converting datetime objects to strings via strftime() and back may lose proper Excel date functionality.
memory_items.generalization: For date operations in spreadsheets, prefer manipulating datetime.date objects directly and letting Excel formatting handle visual presentation via number_format.

edits.content:
## Trace-Derived Lessons

### Date Cell Type Validation
- Always check actual cell data types (datetime, str, int) before applying transformations
- Use isinstance(cell.value, datetime.datetime) to detect datetime objects that may appear as dates
- String parsing logic (split, regex) fails silently on datetime objects

### Excel Number Format Behavior
- number_format controls visual display only, not stored value type
- Cells storing datetime.datetime will use Excel date rendering rules regardless of number_format assignment
- To show dates without time, use datetime.date objects or set time component to zero

### Safe Date Manipulation Pattern
- Extract date from datetime using .date() method when working with datetime objects
- Apply number_format='mm/dd/yyyy' after confirming value type is appropriate
- Verify output by checking both cell.value type AND cell.number_format in post-processing
